text: A large number of Americans hold mixed conservative and liberal views across a wide range of issues and surveys. As has been documented by many researchers, these Americans are characterised as being cross-pressured. In a recent paper titled “Moderates” Fowler et al. claim that rather than having cross-pressured views, most Americans hold centrist views on the individual issues that they have an opinion about. The paper develops a method to decide whether or not an individual's opinions can be fully explained by a centrist position on liberal–conservative scales. These scales capture the views of “a large majority” of the public according to their method and so since a large number of individuals' opinions are estimated to be centred around moderate locations on these scales they must, according to the article, be moderates with centrist opinions on the issues. We claim that Fowler et al's (2023) method for deciding whether or not a voter has centrist opinions amounts to a bias toward assuming that voters' opinions are centred around a moderate location on any liberal-conservative scale. Fowler et al assume that a voter's opinions will be centrist unless their responses can be attributed to randomness or to the fact that the voters' opinions cluster in question fall into one of the distinct clusters identified in the data. We show via simulations that there is an important number of individuals for whom the FET fails to centre their opinions on the mean location of the distribution — so in particular it fails for those whose opinions are randomly distributed around the mean or whose distribution is 'bimodal'. Hence their conclusion that there are a large number of moderate Americans with mixed conservative/liberal attitudes who, as such, must have centrist opinions, is suspect.
